designs.area: Overall theme / palette (--paper cream background + warm crimson-gold-indigo)
designs.problem: The whole page sits on a cream paper background (#f7f4ef) with white cards and a wine-menu crimson/gold/indigo palette. That reads as a refined, traditional, 'grown-up travel brochure' vibe. But the actual content I care about is neon Tokyo, Akihabara arcades, teamLab, Shibuya at night, Super Nintendo World. The cream paper actively fights the hype. It looks like a nice dad PDF, not something made for a 19-year-old. This is the single biggest thing keeping it at 'very good' instead of 'stunning.'
designs.fix: Flip to a dark canvas. Set body{background:#0d1220;color:#eef2f8}. Convert .day cards to dark glass: background:rgba(255,255,255,.04); border:1px solid rgba(255,255,255,.08); backdrop-filter:blur(8px); and lighten body text (.day-body>p color:#c7cede). Keep crimson (#b3352d) and gold (#c8a24a) as neon accents against the dark, and let the day photos glow against it. Recolor the .beats>li from --paper to a subtle rgba(255,255,255,.05) so bullets don't turn into white blocks on dark. Dark makes the neon photos pop instead of being boxed on cream.

designs.area: Hero typography (font-family + JAPAN 2026 in Helvetica 800)
designs.problem: 'JAPAN 2026' is set in Hiragino Sans / Helvetica at weight 800. That's the default system sans everyone has seen a million times, so the hero title has zero personality. A hype trip poster lives or dies on the display type, and right now the biggest words on the page look like a slide-deck header. Nothing about the type says 'this trip is going to be sick.'
designs.fix: Load one display face via <link> and use it only for .hero h1, .week-divider b, .day-title h2, and .next h3. Something heavy and condensed reads as poster/hype: font-family:'Anton',system-ui for those headings, or a wide grotesk like 'Archivo' at 800 with letter-spacing:-.02em. Bump .hero h1 to clamp(44px,9vw,84px), line-height:.95, text-transform:uppercase. Keep body copy on the system stack. This one swap changes the whole first impression.

designs.area: Falling sakura petals canvas (#petals, August trip)
designs.problem: Pink sakura petals drifting over everything is THE most overused 'this is a Japan website' cliche, and it's the first thing that screams dad-made-this. Worse, the trip is August 1-13 and cherry blossoms are an early-April thing, so it's factually the wrong season. And because #petals is z-index:3 (above the deck at z-index:2), the pink petals float on top of the white cards and text as I'm reading, which is just visual noise on content.
designs.fix: Cut the sakura entirely, or make it season-right and subtle. Best move for a gamer: replace the pink petal COLORS array with warm ember/neon specks (['#ffb347','#ff5e5e','#c8a24a']) drifting UP slowly like festival embers, drop the count from 30 to ~14, and set #petals{z-index:1} so it sits BEHIND the .deck cards instead of over your reading. If you keep petals, at minimum put them behind content. Even better: kill it and spend the wow-budget on the hero (see hero finding).

designs.area: Felix's Favorite / anime days get no special treatment
designs.problem: Day 4 'Arcade Heaven' (Akihabara) carries a tiny 'FELIX'S FAVORITE' chip and then looks 100% identical to every other day card. The one day literally built for me, the day I'd screenshot and send to friends, is visually the same as 'Land & Ease In.' The chip is an 11px pill. That's a massive missed hype opportunity on the exact cards that would make me excited.
designs.fix: Give favorite/anime days a distinct visual state. Add a class like .day.fav and style: border:1px solid var(--gold); box-shadow:0 0 0 1px rgba(200,162,74,.4),0 10px 50px rgba(200,162,74,.18); and animate a slow neon pulse on the border via keyframes. Make the .chip bigger and glowing (padding:6px 14px; box-shadow:0 0 16px rgba(200,162,74,.5)) and pin it top-right over the photo instead of buried above the paragraph. Consider a second accent for gamer days (arcade, Super Nintendo World, Den Den Town, teamLab) so scrolling past them feels like hitting the good levels.

designs.area: Hero image + no motion + no countdown
designs.problem: The hero is a still skytree.jpg with a slow Ken Burns zoom. Skytree is the polite, postcard, dad-picks-the-landmark choice, and a static photo (even zooming) doesn't punch. There's also no countdown, no 'X days until Tokyo' hook, nothing that creates urgency or hype. The emotional hero for a teen is neon Shibuya scramble at night or an Akihabara wall of screens, in motion.
designs.fix: Swap the hero bg to a night neon shot (Shibuya crossing or Akihabara) and, if bandwidth allows, use a short muted looping <video autoplay muted loop playsinline> poster-backed clip instead of the jpg for real movement. Add a live countdown under the dates: a small JS that computes days to 2026-08-01 and renders 'Tokyo in 18 days' in gold. A countdown is the single most screenshot-and-send element you can add, and it's ~10 lines of JS.

designs.area: Panel expand interaction + touch feedback
designs.problem: Tapping a bullet just flips the hidden attribute, so panels snap open/closed with zero animation, which feels cheap next to how polished the scroll reveals are. And when a panel with 5-6 spots opens on a phone, nothing scrolls it into view, so the content jumps and I lose my place. On top of that, every interactive element only has :hover states (.lnk:hover, .book a:hover, .day:hover), which do literally nothing on a touchscreen, so taps give no feedback.
designs.fix: Animate the expand: instead of [hidden], drive it with max-height:0;overflow:hidden;opacity:0;transition:max-height .35s ease,opacity .25s ease and toggle a class that sets max-height:2000px;opacity:1 (keep aria-expanded for a11y). In the click handler, after opening, call p.scrollIntoView({behavior:'smooth',block:'nearest'}). Add :active states everywhere you have :hover, e.g. .beats .lnk:active{background:#fbeee0;transform:scale(.99)} and .day:active for a tap-press feel. On touch, active feedback is what makes it feel like an app, not a page.

designs.area: Image loading / layout stability (CLS + weight)
designs.problem: Day photos and gallery images have no width/height or aspect-ratio, so as the lazy JPGs load, cards reflow and the page jumps under my thumb while scrolling, which feels janky and slow. The hero skytree.jpg is a full-bleed JPG with no fetchpriority, and there's no WebP/AVIF anywhere, so on cellular the first paint drags. On a phone the whole thing has to feel instant or it reads as old.
designs.fix: Reserve space: add aspect-ratio to .day-photo img (it's already 260px tall container, so give the width/height attrs or set .day-photo{aspect-ratio:16/10}) and give .pgal figure a fixed aspect-ratio (already fixed height, good). Serve WebP/AVIF via or just re-export the JPGs as WebP; day photos at 260px tall don't need full-res. Add fetchpriority='high' to the hero bg (or preload it) and loading='lazy' only to below-fold images (it already is). Target < 200KB per day photo.

designs.area: Copy tone in footer/subhead reads dad-project-manager
designs.problem: Little text choices undercut the hype: the footer says 'Draft itinerary · built for Felix · nothing locked, everything changeable' and 'Prefer the tap-through version?', and the hero 'for' line is an italic instruction ('Tap any bullet to open the details'). That's project-management voice, not stoke voice. It's the verbal equivalent of the cream background, it makes a genuinely cool itinerary sound like a status update.
designs.fix: Rewrite the micro-copy to sound like the trip, not the doc. Footer: 'Two weeks. Two of us. Tokyo to Osaka. Let's go.' Replace the italic instruction line with something with energy and move the 'tap a bullet' hint to a tiny muted caption near the first card only. Keep 'nothing locked' if Dad wants it, but shrink it and lead with hype, not disclaimers.

designs.area: Typography — headline/display font (hero H1, day titles, week dividers)
designs.problem: The entire site runs on the OS UI stack: font-family:"Hiragino Sans","Helvetica Neue",Helvetica,Arial. No display face is loaded anywhere (no <link> to fonts, no @font-face). 'JAPAN 2026' at 800-weight Helvetica caps and every h2 in Helvetica is the single biggest reason this reads as a competent template rather than an award-tier piece — the wordmark, the moment that should stop a 19-year-old's thumb, is set in the same font as a system settings screen. Helvetica-800 caps with letter-spacing:1px has no personality and no craft.
designs.fix: Load exactly two families and split roles. Headlines/wordmark: preconnect + <link> to a variable editorial display — Fraunces (serif, optical sizing) for a premium/cinematic read, OR Anton / Bebas Neue if you want a bolder poster punch for the teen. Body/labels: Inter or Satoshi. Then: add `--display` and `--text` vars; `.hero h1{font-family:var(--display);font-weight:600;font-optical-sizing:auto;letter-spacing:-.02em;}` (drop the +1px tracking — negative tracking on a large display face reads modern), `.day-title h2,.week-divider .wt b,.next h3{font-family:var(--display);}`, and set body/`.beats`/`.tag`/`.sub` to var(--text). Keep the Japanese kanji glyphs on Hiragino. This one change moves it two tiers.

designs.area: Day captions — undefined .pill class (rendering defect)
designs.problem: Every single day closes with … ('Keep it light · then crash', 'Wheels up · start planning the next one', etc.) but there is NO `.pill` rule in the stylesheet — grep confirms zero CSS for it. So all 13 of these captions render as bare inline body text with no styling, floating under the bullet list. It looks unfinished and inconsistent next to the carefully styled `.chip` badge right above it. This is a straight bug, not a taste call.
designs.fix: Add a real rule, e.g. `.pill{display:inline-block;margin-top:18px;padding:7px 16px;border-radius:999px;background:var(--paper);border:1px solid #e5dccb;color:var(--muted);font-size:12px;font-weight:700;letter-spacing:1.5px;text-transform:uppercase;}`. It's the day's closing line — treat it as a small monospaced or uppercase 'stamp' so it reads as a deliberate sign-off, not a leftover sentence.

designs.area: Load performance — hero + gallery images (iPhone LCP)
designs.problem: img/skytree.jpg (the hero background) is 3.1MB and is set as a CSS `background-image`, so it is render-blocking for the hero and cannot use loading=lazy. The whole img/ folder is 58MB; individual day photos are ~1MB each. On an iPhone over LTE this is a multi-second first paint on the exact screen meant to wow — the kanji animation fires before the photo even arrives. background-image also can't be preloaded normally and gets no responsive sizing.
designs.fix: Compress and convert: export hero + day photos to WebP/AVIF at ~1600px wide, target 150–350KB each (skytree from 3.1MB to ~250KB). Replace the `.hero .bg` CSS background with an actual (object-fit:cover, decoding=async, fetchpriority="high") plus a so it's prioritized. Add width/height (or aspect-ratio) to all to kill layout shift. Consider a tiny blurred LQIP (10px base64) behind the hero so the fade-in never shows the flat #22304f block.

designs.area: Petal canvas — z-index over content, seasonal cliché, motion load
designs.problem: #petals is z-index:3 while .deck content is z-index:2 — so pink sakura petals drift ON TOP of the white day cards and body text for the entire scroll, which cheapens the reading surface and fights legibility. Two more issues: (1) cherry blossoms are a spring signifier but this is an August trip — it's the most default 'Japan = sakura' stock move, which a jury reads as generic; (2) petals + kenburns + pulsing sun + dashed route + riding train + ring pulses is a lot of simultaneous motion competing for attention, closer to busy than premium.
designs.fix: Drop the canvas to z-index:1 (behind .deck) OR confine petals to the hero only and fade them out on scroll past it. Cut petal count from 30 to ~14 and cap opacity lower. Best move for a non-cliché, still-cinematic feel: swap falling sakura for slow-drifting warm embers/lantern-glow motes tied to the crimson/gold palette, or keep petals but only in the hero. Reduce concurrent motion elsewhere — the pulsing `.sun` at 7s and the `ring` pulse on every day-num are the most droppable.

designs.area: Micro-interaction — panel expand is an instant pop, images snap in
designs.problem: Tapping a bullet toggles `panel.hidden`, so the detail block (gallery + spots + tips) appears in one abrupt jump with zero transition — the layout lurches. Then the lazy gallery images set .src and paint with no fade, popping in one by one. For a site whose whole interaction model is tap-to-expand, this is the moment that should feel silky and instead feels like a raw show/hide. The rotating + → × on `.go` is nicely done, but the content reveal it triggers isn't.
designs.fix: Animate the reveal: wrap panel content and transition `grid-template-rows:0fr → 1fr` (the modern clip-free expand) or animate max-height + opacity over ~.35s cubic-bezier(.2,.7,.2,1); toggle a class instead of the `hidden` attribute (keep hidden for a11y until measured). For images, start them at opacity:0 and add `img.onload=()=>img.style.opacity=1` with `transition:opacity .4s` so gallery thumbs fade in instead of snapping.

designs.area: Hero color/depth — dated red-glow text-shadows
designs.problem: The kanji uses text-shadow:0 4px 40px rgba(179,53,45,.7) and h1 uses 0 3px 20px rgba(0,0,0,.5) — large colored glows behind type are a distinctly 2012-era treatment and slightly muddy the crimson kanji against the dark indigo veil (crimson #b3352d on a #141a2c veil is heavy but low-vibrancy). It reads 'PowerPoint drop shadow' rather than editorial.
designs.fix: Kill the crimson glow on `.kanji` — replace with a tight, subtle depth shadow (e.g. text-shadow:0 2px 24px rgba(0,0,0,.45)) and let the crimson stay flat and graphic, or better, render the kanji as a semi-transparent oversized watermark (color:rgba(179,53,45,.9) with -webkit-text-stroke or a thin gold outline) so it feels like a stamp/seal, not a glowing logo. For contrast, deepen the veil bottom slightly or nudge the kanji toward the brighter --crimson so it separates cleanly from the indigo.

designs.area: Navigation / orientation — no sticky chapter cue on a 13-day page
designs.problem: This is a long single scroll (13 day cards, each expandable). The only progress signal is the 4px top bar, which tells you how far but not where (Tokyo vs Kansai). On a phone it's easy to lose your place after opening a few panels, and there's no fast way back to a week.
designs.fix: Add a slim sticky chapter label that swaps on scroll — reuse the IntersectionObserver already in the JS to watch the two `.week-divider`s and update a small fixed top-left pill ('WK 1 · TOKYO' / 'WK 2 · KANSAI'). Optionally make the progress bar two-segment so the Tokyo/Kansai split is visible. Low cost, meaningfully more premium wayfinding.

designs.area: Book-These section — raw monospace URLs look cheap
designs.problem: The two 'Book These Now' cards print the literal URL in ui-monospace with word-break:break-all ('usj.co.jp/web/en/us ↗'). Monospaced broken URLs read as developer/debug output and undercut the otherwise polished dark-gradient CTA section — the least premium element on the page sits in the highest-intent section.
designs.fix: Drop the raw URL `em`. The whole card is already an <a>, so make the affordance a styled button/label instead — e.g. a gold-outline 'Reserve ↗' chip at the card bottom (border:1px solid var(--gold);border-radius:999px;padding:8px 16px;font-weight:700). Keeps the click target, removes the techy string, and gives the section a clear action instead of a printed link.

designs.area: Beat expand/collapse micro-interaction (.panel toggle in JS + .panel CSS)
designs.problem: The single most-used interaction on the whole site — tapping a bullet to reveal its detail panel — has NO animation. The JS just flips the `hidden` attribute (`p.hidden=open`), so the panel snaps open/shut instantly and jerks the scroll position. Only the little `.go` plus-sign rotates. Everything else about the page is animated (kenburns, petals, pop-in day numbers), which makes the one thing the kid actually touches feel the cheapest and most abrupt. This is the difference between 'very good' and 'can't put it down.'
designs.fix: Stop using the `hidden` attribute for visibility and animate height with a grid trick. CSS: `.panel{display:grid;grid-template-rows:0fr;opacity:0;transition:grid-template-rows .38s cubic-bezier(.4,0,.2,1),opacity .28s ease;}` `.panel>*{overflow:hidden;min-height:0;}` (wrap the pgal/pspots/ptips in one inner `<div>` so the single child can clip) and `.panel.open{grid-template-rows:1fr;opacity:1;}`. Remove the `hidden` attributes from the HTML. In JS replace `p.hidden=open` with `p.classList.toggle('open', !open)`. Keep the lazy-img load on first open. Add a matching fade/stagger on the gallery figures with `.panel.open figure{animation:riseIn .4s ease both}` for extra polish.

designs.area: .pill day-caption (used on every day, e.g. 'Keep it light · then crash', line 188 etc.)
designs.problem: The `.pill` class is used at the bottom of all 13 day cards but has ZERO CSS defined anywhere in the stylesheet (confirmed by grep — only usages, no selector). So the closing caption renders as unstyled default inline text: no background, no color, no spacing, sitting flush against the last bullet. It reads as an unfinished element on every single card — the clearest 'amateur/broken' tell on the page.
designs.fix: Add a real style: `.pill{display:inline-block;margin-top:18px;padding:7px 15px;border-radius:999px;background:#fbeee0;color:var(--crimson-deep);font-size:12px;font-weight:800;letter-spacing:.5px;text-transform:uppercase;border:1px solid rgba(179,53,45,.22);}` — matches the warm crimson-on-paper system already used for `.lnk:hover` and ties the card's footer back to the day-number crimson.

designs.area: Hero LCP image (img/skytree.jpg, 3.1 MB, loaded as CSS background on .hero .bg)
designs.problem: The hero — the first thing the son sees and the Largest Contentful Paint — is a 3.1 MB JPEG delivered as a CSS `background-image`. Browsers deprioritize CSS backgrounds, and 3.1 MB over cellular means the veil-dark hero sits blank for seconds before the photo pops in behind the already-animated text. Every other full-bleed photo (shinjuku, ginza, osaka ~1 MB each) is also oversized. On a phone this is the biggest wow-killer: a stunning site that loads slow reads as broken.
designs.fix: Two parts. (1) Resize/recompress: `sips -Z 1800 img/skytree.jpg` then re-encode to ~70% quality (target <350 KB), and ideally ship a WebP. Do the same batch to every img/*.jpg over ~500 KB. (2) Prioritize the hero: add `` in ``, or better, make the hero bg a real `` with `object-fit:cover` so the browser treats it as LCP. Give `.day-photo img` explicit `width`/`height` attributes too, to kill layout shift as they lazy-load.

designs.area: Sakura petal canvas (#petals z-index:3, 30 petals, rAF loop)
designs.problem: Two issues. (1) `#petals` sits at `z-index:3`, ABOVE `.deck` (`z-index:2`), so pink petals fall in front of the white day cards and body text — over a light card they read as floating dust/noise on top of the reading content, not a background effect, and they slightly hurt text legibility. (2) The `requestAnimationFrame` loop runs forever at full rate even when the hero is scrolled far offscreen and when the tab is backgrounded — needless battery/heat on an iPhone during a long 13-day scroll.
designs.fix: (1) Drop the canvas behind content: `#petals{z-index:1}` (deck stays 2, progress bar 50). Petals then read as atmosphere behind the paper, which is the intent. (2) Pause the loop: in `tick()` early-return when `document.hidden` and re-kick on `visibilitychange`; and stop animating once `scrollY > innerHeight*1.2` (hero gone) by not scheduling the next frame, restarting on scroll back up. Also drop the count from 30 to ~16 — on mobile it's plenty and halves the per-frame cost.

designs.area: Duplicate panel content across different bullets (Day 10 Universal, Day 12 Hiroshima, Day 13)
designs.problem: Several distinct bullets reuse another bullet's exact gallery + spot list. Day 10 beat 2 'Power-Up Band battle' shows the identical 3 photos and 5 spots as beat 1 'Super Nintendo World' (lines 302). Day 12 beat 3 'Hiroshima okonomiyaki' reuses the Peace Memorial panel verbatim (line 326). Day 13's 'market breakfast' and 'farewell ramen' reuse Kuromon/ramen panels from Day 1/11. When the kid taps two different-sounding bullets and gets pixel-identical photos and text, the site reads as lazy/broken and kills trust in the rest of the detail.
designs.fix: Give each of these beats its own `.pgal` and `.pspots`: Power-Up Band battle -> band/app/scoreboard shots + spots; Hiroshima okonomiyaki -> Okonomimura griddle shots + a couple of specific shops; Day 13 breakfast/ramen -> distinct images. If unique content isn't worth it, collapse the redundant bullet so each day has only genuinely distinct expandables (e.g. fold 'Power-Up Band battle' into the Super Nintendo World bullet).

designs.area: Touch feedback / hover-only states (.beats .lnk:hover, .book a:hover, .day)
designs.problem: All interactive feedback is `:hover`, which doesn't exist on an iPhone — so tapping a bullet gives no press feedback at all, and on iOS a tapped element can get stuck in the `:hover` background until you tap elsewhere. There's also no `-webkit-tap-highlight-color` reset, so taps flash the default grey iOS box over your custom rounded buttons. The whole page is 'viewed mostly on an iPhone' yet has no real tap feel.
designs.fix: Add `body{-webkit-tap-highlight-color:transparent;}`. Wrap hover rules in `@media(hover:hover){ .beats .lnk:hover{...} .book a:hover{...} }` so touch devices don't get stuck states. Add explicit press states: `.beats .lnk:active{background:#f7ddc4;} .book a:active{transform:scale(.98);} ` with `transition:transform .12s ease` on `.book a`. Small immediate feedback on tap is what makes it feel native.

designs.area: Expand affordance + gold '+' contrast (.beats .go)
designs.problem: The only signal that a bullet is tappable is a gold `+` glyph (`--gold #c8a24a`) on the paper card background (`#f7f4ef`) — that's roughly a 2:1 contrast ratio, below the 3:1 minimum for a meaningful UI graphic, so it's easy to miss that these bullets even open. A first-time viewer (the son) may scroll past 13 cards never realizing each bullet expands into the good stuff.
designs.fix: Turn the `+` into a filled, higher-contrast tap target: `.beats .go{width:24px;height:24px;line-height:22px;border-radius:50%;background:var(--crimson);color:#fff;font-size:16px;text-align:center;}` and rotate it on open (`.lnk[aria-expanded='true'] .go{transform:rotate(45deg);background:var(--crimson-deep);}`). Crimson-on-white clears contrast and reads unmistakably as 'tap me.' Optionally add a one-time cue in the hero sub ('Tap any bullet to open the details' already exists — good) and a faint `chevron`/`Tap` label on the first card only.

designs.area: Typography — system Helvetica for all display type (.hero h1, .day-title h2, .week-divider .jp, .hero .kanji)
designs.problem: Every headline including 'JAPAN 2026', the day titles, and the big kanji is just `font-weight:800` Helvetica/Hiragino system sans. It's clean but generic — the exact stock look that reads 'template.' For a 19-year-old the hero especially wants a distinctive display face; right now the wow comes entirely from the photo and motion, and the type does no work.
designs.fix: Add one characterful display face (self-hosted or Google Fonts with `font-display:swap`, preconnected). Example: 'Anton' or 'Archivo Expanded' for `.hero h1` and `.day-title h2` (tight, poster-y, teen-facing), and 'Zen Kaku Gothic New' 900 for `.hero .kanji` + `.week-divider .jp` so the Japanese glyphs look intentional rather than default-rendered. Keep body copy on the current system stack for load speed. Just theming the two heading selectors lifts the whole page out of template territory.

designs.area: Navigation across 13 cards (sticky day-jump nav)
designs.problem: This is one continuous ~13-card page. Each .day is a 210px photo + intro + three collapsible panels (a fully-open day is several screens tall), and the ONLY navigation aid is the 4px #progress hairline at the very top. There is no way to jump to a day, no week anchors, no 'back to top.' On a phone, finding 'Day 9 / Osaka' means blind thumb-flinging past everything, and once you open panels the scroll length balloons unpredictably. This is the single biggest phone-usability gap.
designs.fix: Add a horizontal, edge-to-edge sticky chip bar directly under the hero: position:sticky;top:0;z-index:40;display:flex;overflow-x:auto;gap:8px;scroll-snap-type:x on a strip of <a href='#day-4'> chips (Day 1..13, or Week One / Week Two group headers). Give every <article class='day'> an id (id='day-1'…) and add .day{scroll-margin-top:64px} so smooth-scroll lands below the bar. Sync an 'active' chip via the existing IntersectionObserver. Optionally auto-scroll the active chip into view. Keep the 4px progress bar pinned under this bar.

designs.area: Display typography / wow-factor
designs.problem: Every headline (hero H1 'JAPAN 2026', .day-title h2, .week-divider, .next h3) renders in system Hiragino/Helvetica bold. It's clean but it's the exact stock-sans a strong build defaults to — nothing here reads as a designed, memorable identity, which is what makes a 19-year-old go 'whoa.' The Latin display type is the weakest link versus the strong photography and the crimson/gold palette.
designs.fix: Load one distinctive display face for headlines only, body stays system. Add <link rel='preconnect' href='https://fonts.gstatic.com' crossorigin> + a font like 'Zen Kaku Gothic New' (900) or a condensed display such as 'Archivo Expanded'/'Anton', font-display:swap. Apply to .hero h1, .week-divider .wt b, .day-title h2, .next h3 only. Tighten with letter-spacing:-0.5px on the big H1. Keep --paper body copy on Hiragino for legibility.

designs.area: Expand/collapse interaction
designs.problem: The panel toggle is a raw `p.hidden=open` flip — the panel appears/disappears instantly with zero height animation, and lazy images inside load AFTER it opens, so the card jumps twice (first the panel snaps in, then the 3-image gallery decodes and shoves everything below down). If you're mid-scroll with your thumb, the content you were reading jumps out from under you. There's also no scroll-into-view, so opening a panel low on a tall card can push the button you tapped off-screen.
designs.fix: Animate the reveal instead of toggling `hidden`: wrap panel contents and transition grid-template-rows:0fr→1fr (or max-height) over ~250ms cubic-bezier(.2,.7,.2,1). Kick the lazy-load one frame BEFORE the open animation and call img.decode() so the gallery is sized before it expands (kills the second jump). After opening, requestAnimationFrame(()=>btn.scrollIntoView({block:'nearest',behavior:'smooth'})). Set the panel figures' height explicitly (already fixed at 120/150px) so reserved space matches — good, keep that.

designs.area: Petal canvas — performance & layering
designs.problem: #petals is a fixed full-screen canvas at z-index:3 (ABOVE .deck at z-index:2) running requestAnimationFrame forever, even off-screen and when the tab is backgrounded — a steady battery/GPU draw on a phone across a long scroll session, and a fixed continuously-repainting layer over 13 image cards is exactly what causes scroll jank on older iPhones. It's also not DPR-scaled (c.width=innerWidth), so petals render soft/blurry on Retina. And drifting pink sakura over every white card the whole way down reads a bit dated/gimmicky and slightly lowers card-text contrast.
designs.fix: Drop z-index to 1 so petals sit BEHIND the white cards (cleaner, better text contrast, still visible on the dark hero). Scale for DPR: const d=Math.min(devicePixelRatio,2); c.width=innerWidth*d; c.height=innerHeight*d; x.scale(d,d). Pause the loop on document.hidden via visibilitychange (and cancelAnimationFrame). Reduce count to ~16 when innerWidth<600. Consider fading petals out past the hero (opacity ramp on scroll) so they're an intro flourish, not a full-page overlay.

designs.area: Hero viewport height + LCP image load
designs.problem: Two phone-specific hits at first paint. (1) .hero uses min-height:92vh; on iOS Safari vh is the tall viewport, so when the URL bar collapses on first scroll the hero visibly resizes/reflows — a jump right at the most important moment. (2) The hero background is a CSS background-image('img/skytree.jpg'), which the browser discovers late (after CSS parse), so the LCP element loads slowly on cellular and the crimson kanji sits over an empty dark veil for a beat.
designs.fix: Use min-height:92svh with a 92vh line just above it as fallback. Preload the LCP image in : . If feasible, serve skytree.jpg as a real in the hero (object-fit:cover) so fetchpriority applies natively rather than a background layer.

designs.area: Day-photo image loading (navy-box flash + CLS-free but poppy)
designs.problem: Every .day-photo sits on a solid #22304f box and every img is loading='lazy'. As each of the 13 cards scrolls up, you get a flash of flat navy that then hard-cuts to the photo when it decodes — 13 times down the page it feels cheap and unfinished. The first card's photo is also lazy, so it's blank navy even though it's near the top.
designs.fix: Add .day-photo img{opacity:0;transition:opacity .4s ease} and set .loaded{opacity:1} via an onload handler (or the JS reveal), so photos fade in instead of hard-cutting. Set the FIRST day card's img to loading='eager' fetchpriority='high'. Optional but high-polish: a tiny blurred LQIP as the .day-photo background so the fade is photo-to-photo, not navy-to-photo.

designs.area: Gallery thumbnails are dead-ends (micro-interaction / teen appeal)
designs.problem: Inside each panel the .pgal is three 120–150px thumbnails that do nothing on tap. A 19-year-old's instinct on a phone is to tap a photo to see it big and swipe through — right now the most visual, exciting content is stuck at postage-stamp size with no payoff. It reads as filler rather than a gallery.
designs.fix: Add a lightweight lightbox: click a figure img → a fixed full-screen overlay (background rgba(20,26,44,.95)) showing that image contain-fit, tap/swipe-down or an X to close, arrow/swipe to move within the day's set. ~30 lines of JS, no library. Give the figures cursor:pointer and role='button'. This is a cheap, big perceived-quality jump on the exact content teens engage with.

designs.area: Label treatment monotony / template-y hierarchy
designs.problem: The gold + UPPERCASE + wide letter-spacing eyebrow treatment is used seven different ways — .hero .sub, .day-title .tag, .week-divider span, .ptips h4, .scroll-hint, .route-stops, .day-num small. When the same micro-label style repeats everywhere it stops signaling hierarchy and starts looking like a template default; nothing feels primary.
designs.fix: Reserve gold-uppercase-letterspaced for true section eyebrows (week divider, ptips heading) only. Change .day-title .tag to sentence case, lighter weight, softer color (e.g. rgba(255,255,255,.85), letter-spacing:.5px, text-transform:none) so the place/date reads as a caption under the title rather than competing with it. This immediately de-templatizes the card headers.

remixes.remixTitle: Two Laps of the Mushroom Kingdom
remixes.vibe: SNW is already the dream day - so squeeze every drop out of it and don't let the park close the fun.
remixes.why: The base plan nails it; a 19yo gamer's only real enemy at Universal is the 90-min queues and calling it quits at park close. Buy the line-skip, hit the newest zone, and turn the night into round two.

alts.name: Universal Express Pass 4 or 7 (SNW timed entry)
alts.blurb: August is peak-heat and peak-crowd; the Express Pass + a Nintendo World area ticket is the difference between 3 rides and 10. Book the earliest SNW entry slot so you're on Mario Kart before the mob.

alts.name: Donkey Kong Country expansion
alts.blurb: The zone that opened Dec 2024 - Mine-Cart Madness coaster, Kong-themed everything, way shorter lines than the Mario side early. Do it first, then loop back to Mario.

alts.name: Power-Up Band + Bowser's Challenge AR run
alts.blurb: Turn the dad-vs-son band battle into a full-park key-hunt: punch blocks, beat the mini-games, race the AR Mario Kart ride twice to swap who wins.

alts.name: Night lap: Hollywood Dream / Flying Dinosaur + Dotonbori
alts.blurb: SNW glows neon after dark - stay for the Mario area lit up, ride the big coasters with no midday wait, then hit Dotonbori for a late takoyaki victory dinner.

remixes.vibe: Osaka's Akihabara, but grittier and cheaper - retro-hunt all day, arcade all night, no filler.
remixes.why: This is basically Felix's second favorite day. Don't dilute it with markets - stack the retro shops, the anime flagships, and the 8-floor Round1 into one gamer marathon and add a night arcade tournament vibe.

alts.name: Super Potato Osaka + Retro TV Game Shop crawl
alts.blurb: Den Den Town's Nipponbashi strip is wall-to-wall retro: Famicom carts, boxed Game Boys, CRT bargains. Cheaper and less picked-over than Akiba - real hunting ground.

alts.name: Round1 Stadium 8 floors + VS Park
alts.blurb: Namba's mega-arcade: UFO catchers, rhythm games, bowling, plus Bandai's VS Park for full-body sports games. Do a dad-vs-son claw-machine and rhythm-game showdown to close the night.

alts.name: Pokemon Center Osaka + Jump Store + Mandarake Grand Chaos
alts.blurb: Pokemon Center at Daimaru Umeda, the Shonen Jump flagship, and Mandarake's Umeda floors for vintage figures/doujin - the anime-merch trifecta.

alts.name: Kuromon Market lunch (kept, but fast) → Gachapon Kaikan run
alts.blurb: Grab grilled scallop and wagyu skewers at Kuromon, then blow pocket change on a wall of gachapon machines - the perfect low-stakes souvenir sweep.

remixes.remixTitle: Castle or Bust: The Flex Day Fork
remixes.vibe: Hiroshima is heavy and far - either commit to it with the fun Miyajima half, or bail and go full samurai-and-neon instead.
remixes.why: The Peace Park is genuinely worth doing, but it's a somber 2.5hr round trip on the bullet train. A 19yo will get more out of it paired with Miyajima's deer + mountain ropeway - or, if the vibe wants a lift, swapped for a real castle and a wild retro-Osaka afternoon.

alts.name: Keep Hiroshima, lead with Miyajima
alts.blurb: Do the Peace Museum in the morning, then ferry to Miyajima for the floating torii, more bowing deer, and the Mount Misen ropeway with island views - ends on a high, not a heavy note.

alts.name: Swap for Himeji Castle (samurai fan's dream)
alts.blurb: It's right on the Osaka-Hiroshima shinkansen line: Japan's biggest surviving original castle, the real-deal fortress that skinned a hundred anime backdrops. Half the travel time of Hiroshima.

alts.name: Full Osaka stay-day: Shinsekai + Spa World + Tsutenkaku
alts.blurb: Skip the train entirely - retro Shinsekai backstreets, kushikatsu, the Tsutenkaku tower slide, and Spa World's themed onsen floors for a father-son soak.

alts.name: Second Universal half-day (Express evening ticket)
alts.blurb: If SNW left them wanting more, a discounted twilight ticket gets you back for the rides they missed with near-zero lines after 4pm.

remixes.remixTitle: Last Boss: Osaka Castle, Then Loot the Store
remixes.vibe: One iconic morning view, then a fast souvenir-and-gachapon sweep before wheels-up.
remixes.why: Osaka Castle is a solid last-morning postcard, but a gamer's real last-day mission is grabbing the merch he'll regret leaving behind. Keep the castle short and reserve energy for a final loot run timed to the flight.

alts.name: Osaka Castle exterior + Miraiza grounds (fast version)
alts.blurb: Do the moat, the golden-trimmed keep, and a photo from the plaza in under an hour - skip the museum-line inside unless the flight's late. Free samurai-armor photo spots on the grounds.

alts.name: Kuromon or Dotonbori last market breakfast
alts.blurb: Final round of takoyaki, fresh uni, and a farewell tonkotsu ramen - eat the things you'll crave on the plane home.

alts.name: Don Quijote Dotonbori + Ebisu Tower ferris wheel
alts.blurb: The multi-floor Donki is the ultimate last-minute souvenir dump - snacks, Kit-Kats, gadgets - and the oval ferris wheel bolted to it gives one last skyline hit.

alts.name: Final gachapon + Pokemon Center loot run
alts.blurb: Blow the last of the yen coins on gachapon and grab the Osaka-exclusive Pokemon Center merch before heading to the airport - the closer with zero regret.

remixes.remixTitle: Kyoto Has a Save Point Too
remixes.vibe: The bullet train is the ride, and Kyoto's nerd underbelly is the reward.
remixes.why: A temple-and-tea arrival day reads sleepy to a 19yo. Frame the Shinkansen itself as the event, then hit the fact that Kyoto is Nintendo's actual hometown plus a stacked manga/arcade scene right off Kawaramachi, so his gamer brain has something to latch onto before the shrine grind starts.

alts.name: Ekiben + Mt Fuji seat (E, right side)
alts.blurb: Grab a station bento before boarding and book the two-seat side; Fuji blasts past around the 40-min mark for a photo at 175 mph.

alts.name: Kyoto International Manga Museum
alts.blurb: A converted old schoolhouse with 50,000 manga you pull off the walls and read on the lawn; easily 2 hours if he's into it.

alts.name: Teramachi / Shinkyogoku arcade crawl
alts.blurb: Covered shopping arcades off Nishiki packed with gachapon walls, claw machines, and retro game shops for a UFO-catcher run after lunch.

alts.name: Marufukuro (original Nintendo HQ)
alts.blurb: Nintendo's 1889 playing-card headquarters, now a boutique hotel; a quick pilgrimage photo stop for a kid who grew up on Mario.

remixes.vibe: Completionist mode: don't quit at the viewpoint, summit the whole mountain.
remixes.why: This day is already gorgeous, so don't replace it, harden it. A gamer respects a full-clear, and 90% of tourists bail on Fushimi Inari at the halfway viewpoint. Send him to the actual summit, add a samurai-armor photo, and reveal the gates are open 24/7 so a night return is genuinely eerie and empty.

alts.name: Fushimi Inari full summit loop
alts.blurb: Push past the Yotsutsuji viewpoint (where everyone stops) all the way to Ichinomine at the top, ~2-3 hrs round trip for the real bragging rights.

alts.name: Samurai armor / kimono photo walk
alts.blurb: Rent full armor or a yukata at a shop near Kiyomizu-dera and shoot in the Higashiyama lanes; peak main-character energy.

alts.name: Night return to Fushimi Inari
alts.blurb: The torii are lit and near-empty after dark and the shrine never closes; a genuinely spooky, movie-tier walk with zero crowds.

alts.name: Ninja/samurai experience add-on
alts.blurb: A hands-on katana-drawing or shuriken-throwing session at a Higashiyama dojo if the legs still have energy before dinner.

remixes.vibe: Get mobbed by bowing deer, then walk real anime locations along the river.
remixes.why: Nara deer already sell themselves, but the sleeper hook is that Uji is the actual setting of Sound! Euphonium, a huge anime pilgrimage spot. Add the Todai-ji nostril-pillar challenge and it turns a temple-and-tea day into something he'll actually post about.

alts.name: Todai-ji nostril pillar challenge
alts.blurb: Squeeze through the hole carved the size of the Great Buddha's nostril; clearing it supposedly grants enlightenment, jamming halfway grants a photo.

alts.name: Uji 'Sound! Euphonium' pilgrimage
alts.blurb: Walk the real riverbank, bridge, and Keihan station backdrops from the anime; even non-fans get why Uji is a fan mecca.

alts.name: Shika senbei deer gauntlet
alts.blurb: Buy the deer crackers and get swarmed; hold one over your head and they'll actually bow before they nip you.

alts.name: Byodo-in Phoenix Hall
alts.blurb: The temple printed on the back of the 10-yen coin; pull one out and match it in person before the matcha stop.

remixes.vibe: Monkeys and moss in the morning, then step straight into a video-game city at night.
remixes.why: The bamboo-and-gold morning is fine, but the payoff is the Osaka arrival: Dotonbori is the direct real-world model for Sotenbori in the Yakuza / Like a Dragon games. For a gamer walking into that neon canyon at night, it's literally stepping inside a game he's played. That reframe makes the whole transfer day hype.

alts.name: Iwatayama Monkey Park
alts.blurb: A 20-min hike above Arashiyama where you feed wild macaques from inside a hut (you're caged, they're free) with a full Kyoto panorama.

alts.name: Dotonbori as Sotenbori
alts.blurb: Treat the first night as a Yakuza walking tour: find the Glico running man, the giant Kani Doraku crab, and Ebisu-bashi bridge IRL.

alts.name: Arashiyama e-bike loop
alts.blurb: Rent bikes to hit the bamboo grove, Togetsukyo bridge, and the quiet moss temples faster and ahead of the tour crowds.

alts.name: Ebisu Tower Ferris wheel
alts.blurb: The oval Don Quijote wheel right on the Dotonbori canal for a first-night neon overview before the takoyaki crawl.

remixes.vibe: Ease-in night, but every stop is a gamer/anime pin instead of just wandering.
remixes.why: After a 12+ hour flight he doesn't need a big day, but a 19yo's version of 'Shinjuku night' is arcades, a giant Godzilla, and a rooftop skyline, not a quiet stroll. Everything here is a 10-min walk from Shinjuku Station so it's zero-effort on tired legs.

alts.name: GiGO / Taito Station Kabukicho
alts.blurb: Multi-floor arcade steps from Omoide Yokocho: UFO catchers, rhythm games (maimai/Chunithm), fighting cabinets. Perfect low-energy first hit of Japan gaming.

alts.name: Godzilla Head, Hotel Gracery Kabukicho
alts.blurb: Life-size Godzilla head roars over the Toho building on the hour after dark. Free, dumb, iconic first-night photo.

alts.name: Shibuya Sky at night
alts.blurb: Open-air rooftop deck 229m up; book the ~9pm slot for the full sea-of-neon shot. Better than daytime for a first-night wow.

alts.name: Karaoke Kan, Shibuya
alts.blurb: The 'Lost in Translation' spot; private room, anime/game openings loaded, no drinking needed. Classic dad-son ice-breaker after landing.

remixes.remixTitle: Temples in the Morning, Go-Karts by Lunch
remixes.vibe: Do Senso-ji early, then hijack the afternoon for Mario-kart-through-real-streets and an otaku mega-mall.
remixes.why: Asakusa + Skytree can be done by noon. A 19yo's best-in-life Tokyo memory isn't a second observation tower, it's dressing as a game character and go-karting past actual Tokyo traffic with his dad in the next kart. Note: Street Kart needs a valid license + International Driving Permit, so line that up before the trip.

alts.name: Street Kart Asakusa (real-road go-karting)
alts.blurb: Costumed go-kart tour on public streets past Senso-ji and the river; dad vs son, GoPro footage included. The single most 'I can't believe this is legal' thing he'll do.

alts.name: Nakano Broadway
alts.blurb: 4 floors of Mandarake: vintage figures, retro Famicom carts, cels, trading cards. Grittier and cheaper than Akihabara, way fewer tourists. Swap in for the Skytree deck.

alts.name: Pokemon Center + Nintendo goods, Skytree Town (Solamachi)
alts.blurb: If you keep Skytree, skip the pricey deck and hit the Pokemon Center and character shops at its base instead.

alts.name: Samurai Ninja Museum, Asakusa
alts.blurb: Hands-on: wear armor, throw ninja stars, swing a katana. Cheesy in the best way and a great father-son photo set right by the temple.

remixes.remixTitle: Shibuya Player Select: Sneakers, Nintendo, Streetwear
remixes.vibe: Keep Meiji Shrine calm, then turn Shibuya/Harajuku into a flagship-store and thrift crawl aimed at a gamer who dresses.
remixes.why: Takeshita Street candy is aimed at 14yo girls; a 19yo guy wants the Nintendo/Pokemon/Jump flagships, the sneaker and vintage scene in the back streets, and the rooftop. Same neighborhood, just the version that hits for him.

alts.name: Nintendo Tokyo + Pokemon Center Shibuya (Parco 6F)
alts.blurb: Japan's only official Nintendo store plus a Pokemon Center and a Capcom Store on the same floor. Basically a shrine for him.

alts.name: Jump Shop, Shibuya
alts.blurb: Official Shonen Jump store: One Piece, Dragon Ball, Demon Slayer, Jujutsu Kaisen merch you can't get at home.

alts.name: Ura-Harajuku / Cat Street thrift + sneaker crawl
alts.blurb: Back-alley Harajuku: BAPE, atmos, WEGO, Kinji vintage, Kailua. Where actual Tokyo teens shop, not the tourist candy street.

alts.name: Shimokitazawa detour
alts.blurb: Two stops from Shibuya: vintage-clothing capital, tiny record and game shops, chill cafes. Add if he's into thrifting streetwear.

remixes.vibe: His favorite day is already right; this stacks the deep cuts so it goes from tourist Akiba to real Akiba, plus a night session.
remixes.why: Super Potato and the main drag are the appetizer. The stuff that makes hardcore gamers lose their minds is the shmup/fighting arcade Hey, the figure towers, and retro-PC basements, none of which the average tourist finds. Keep Ueno as a mellow morning, then go all-in on Akiba through the night.

alts.name: Hey (Hirose Entertainment Yard)
alts.blurb: The legendary hardcore arcade: shoot-em-ups, Street Fighter/Guilty Gear cabinets, serious local players. Where the real Akiba gaming happens.

alts.name: Mandarake Complex + Radio Kaikan
alts.blurb: 8 floors of used figures, manga, retro carts (Mandarake) plus the figure/model tower Radio Kaikan. Retro-hunting endgame beyond just Super Potato.

alts.name: Yodobashi Akiba + BEEP retro PC basement
alts.blurb: Yodobashi is a 9-floor electronics/gunpla/game monster; BEEP is a basement shrine to retro PCs, dojin games, and oddball hardware.

alts.name: Night add-on: gachapon hall + Akiba neon + arcade tournament
alts.blurb: After dark hit the GiGO gachapon floors (hundreds of capsule machines) and check if any weekend fighting-game tourney is running. Akiba lit up at night is the real photo.

remixes.vibe: Swap Ginza shopping for Odaiba: teamLab Planets, a 20m Gundam, and Sega's indoor theme park, all one bay-side loop.
remixes.why: teamLab is a lock, but Planets in Toyosu (barefoot, wade through water, the mirror rooms) photographs harder than Ginza's luxury blocks, which mean nothing to a 19yo. It's a short ride from Odaiba, so you chain it with a life-size Gundam and Joypolis. Keep a nice dinner, just move it bayside.

alts.name: teamLab Planets, Toyosu
alts.blurb: The barefoot, walk-through-water version of teamLab; the infinity crystal room and floating-flower dome are the money shots. Book a timed slot ahead.

alts.name: Unicorn Gundam Statue + Gundam Base, DiverCity Odaiba
alts.blurb: 20m Gundam that transforms with a light-and-sound show after dark; Gundam Base inside is the biggest gunpla store on earth. Non-negotiable for an anime guy.

alts.name: Tokyo Joypolis, Odaiba
alts.blurb: Sega's indoor theme park: VR rides, arcade racers, motion coasters. Rainy-day-proof and a genuine dad-vs-son competition zone.

alts.name: Miraikan (National Museum of Emerging Science)
alts.blurb: Robotics, ASIMO's legacy, a giant OLED globe, hands-on future tech. Swap in if he leans more tech-nerd than crowds.
